finding_results.evidence: chapters/chapter_14.md:9-17 (Carmen exchange at nurse's station after count), chapters/chapter_14.md:31-35 (Harry exchange on Wing A)
finding_results.notes: Replaced summary of Carmen's evasiveness with a five-line live exchange at the nurse's station: Tammy asks about Chen (contraction 'What's'), Carmen's hands slow before she retreats into clinical language ('declining fluids,' 'documenting intake'), Tammy pins a number to it (contraction 'That's'), Carmen deflects with badge adjustment and collegial 'You know how it gets,' Tammy files it with 'Sure.' Carmen's lag, procedural retreat, and badge adjustment are all character-specific tells from the style bible, not generic deflection. Replaced summary of Harry's lucid moment with a two-line live exchange: Tammy asks 'You need anything, Mr. Z?' and Harry responds 'No, I'm fine. Thank you, Tammy' — short, declarative, moderately contracted, from the edge of the bed not the wheelchair, with motor control that contradicts his care plan. The contrast with his next-morning Eleanor performance is established on the page. Both exchanges let suspicion arise from spoken texture rather than narrator explanation.

summary: Added two live dialogue exchanges to address the finding that Tammy's suspicion was built from paraphrased rather than on-page dialogue. A five-line Carmen-Tammy exchange at the nurse's station (lines 9-17) shows Carmen's evasive lag and procedural retreat in her own contracted voice, with her badge-adjustment stress tell. A two-line Harry-Tammy exchange on Wing A (lines 31-35) renders Harry's real register live — short, finished, name-using — and contrasts it with his performance mode. Tammy speaks in her high-contraction, flat observational register throughout. Carmen and Harry are voice-separated: Carmen deflects through clinical specificity, Harry through clean brevity. No sprawl introduced; chapter grew from 40 to 62 lines, all additions are dialogue or minimal framing narration.
